verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: “I will now come to an event which he always concealed, owing to the character of the times, but which he could not conceal from us.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: In the matter referred to, there is this of a miraculous nature, that an angel conversed, face to face, with him.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: The Emperor Maximus, while in other respects doubtless a good man, was led astray by the advices of some priests after Priscillian had been put to death.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: He, therefore, protected by his royal power Ithacius the bishop, who had been the accuser of Priscillian, and others of his confederates, whom it is not necessary to name.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: The emperor thus prevented every one from bringing it as a charge against Ithacius, that, by his instrumentality, a man of any sort had been condemned to death.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: Now Martin, constrained to go to the court by many serious causes of people involved in suffering, incurred the whole force of the storm which was there raging.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: The bishops who had assembled at Treves were retained in that city, and daily communicating with Ithacius, they had made common cause with him.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: When it was announced to them expecting no such information, that Martin was coming, completely losing courage, they began to mutter and tremble among themselves.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: And it so happened that already, under their influence, the emperor had determined to send some tribunes armed with absolute power into the two Spains, to search out heretics, and, when found, to deprive them of their life or goods.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: Now there was no doubt that that tempest would also make havoc of multitudes of the real saints, little distinction being made between the various classes of individuals.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: For in such circumstances, a judgment was formed simply by appearances, so that one was deemed a heretic rather on his turning pale from fear, or wearing a particular garment, than by the faith which he professed.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: And the bishops were well aware that such proceedings would by no means please Martin; but, conscious of evil as they were, this was a subject of deep anxiety to them, lest when he came, he should keep from communion with them; knowing well as they did, that others would not be wanting who, with his example to guide them, would follow the bold course adopted by so great a man.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: They therefore form a plan with the emperor, to this effect, that, officials of the court being sent on to meet him, Martin should be forbidden to come any nearer to that city, unless he should declare that he would maintain peace with the bishops who were living there.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: But he skillfully frustrated their object, by declaring that he would come among them with the peace of Christ.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: And at last, having entered during the night, he went to the church, simply for the purpose of prayer.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: On the following day he betakes himself to the palace.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: Besides many other petitions which he had to present, and which it would be tedious to describe, the following were the principal: entreaties in behalf of the courtier Narses, and the president Leucadius, both of whom had belonged to the party of Gratianus, and that, with more than ordinary zeal, upon which this is not the time to dilate, and who had thus incurred the anger of the conqueror; but his chief request was, that tribunes, with the power of life and death, should not be sent into the Spains.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: For Martin felt a pious solicitude not only to save from danger the true Christians in these regions, who were to be persecuted in connection with that expedition, but to protect even heretics themselves.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: But on the first and second day the wily emperor kept the holy man in suspense, whether that he might impress on him the importance of the affair, or because, being obnoxious to the bishops, he could not be reconciled to them, or because, as most people thought at the time, the emperor opposed his wishes from avarice, having cast a longing eye on the property of the persons in question.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: For we are told that he was really a man distinguished by many excellent actions, but that he was not successful in contending against avarice.

verses.book: Dialogue III. The Virtues of Martin Continued.
verses.text: This may, however, have been due to the necessities of the empire at the time, for the treasury of the state had been exhausted by former rulers; and he, being almost constantly in the expectation of civil wars, or in a state of preparation for them, may easily be excused for having, by all sorts of expedients, sought resources for the defense of the empire.
